sentences.text: Yes, well--
Yes.
I only-- I permitted myself-- well, that, that went soon for a very, very long time in my life that I never permitted myself more than four hours sleep because I didn't believe in sleeping.
I believed that once-- I wanted-- I wanted to do so many things that I never had enough time to do them.
I'm still like that, but now I sleep a bit more.
I wanted to tell something which happened which I think is quite important to tell-- to tell about.
In 1941, we were occupied for three days.
The town was occupied for three days by Germans.
Suddenly, tanks and soldiers appeared on the main square.
And we lived just behind-- well, it's a side of the main square.
And they were there for three days.
And during these three days, the Jews were beaten up.
And law came out that Jews could only go to the market, Jewish women, could only go to the market after 10 o'clock when practically every thing was-- that was good was sold already.
After six o'clock, we were not allowed to go out into the street.
And, and this, we didn't know that it would only last three days.
They were on their way to Yugoslavia.
And they stopped in our town for three days.
Now when they left, the restriction of going out in the evening was lifted.
But Hungarians quite often beat up Jews even so.
And the other thing which happened for quite a while, they didn't lift the regulations.
And that was the only town where it happened in Hungary, this, this regulation about the market, that one couldn't go-- that the women couldn't go to the market, only after 10 o'clock.
And that was no lifted.
Altogether, it was a very, very, very anti-Semitic town.
According to the Jewish Encyclopedia and the Encyclopedia Judaica, it was the most anti-Semitic town in Hungary.
I don't know.
I only found that out later.
Szombathely, yes, that's right.
It was a bishop's seat, a bishopry.
And it was quite a well-known town.
It was in the-- the-- it was actually Sa-- Savaria during the Roman times.
And perhaps it's of interest.
When I was a child, about 11, 11, that's when they discovered that we had two Roman roads and a basilica.
And they started to do excavations.
And I was incredibly excited about it.
And I used to take all, all visitors, every visitor, very excitedly to show them.
And I, I nearly went there every day, because I was so interested and so excited by it, these excavations.
Because it was-- Dan-- Danube-- Pannonia was a Roman province.
And Savaria was-- which was Szombathely was before-- I mean, Szombathely-- it became Szombathely-- was the capital of the province.
From some teachers at school, yes.
I don't remember having any anti-Semitism from pupils, from co-schoolmates.
I actually thought about it very much because four years ago, I was invited to go back to the 50th anniversary of school finishing.
And when I wrote that I think they are mistaken, that I really didn't finish school, they said they knew that, but they still, please, would I come?
And they have such nice memories of me.
And they would like me to come back.
And eventually, I went.
And because I started to think that I really didn't have any bad memories regarding my schoolmates.
I had bad memories from, from teachers, especially my history teacher, which was, again, I mean, it's a different story.
It's a--
When I was 13, I was-- no, 13?
I think so, about 13-- I was very much in love with a boy whom I used to see.
We were next to a boys' school.
And I used to see him going into the school.
He was a Jewish boy.
And I thought he was wonderful.
I never spoke to him.
I never met him.
In my hometown, you weren't allowed to speak to boys, not even in groups, unless there was an adult there.
Who's law-- who's regulation was that?
School.
School regulation.
School regulations.
So later on, we used to meet at five o'clock in the morning and in the backs-- back streets and go out on bicycle into different villages, which was much more dangerous and mu-- really stupid.
But we were not allowed to speak to them.
But anyway, I didn't meet him officially.
I didn't speak to him, but I was in love with him.
And one break-- and all my friends, school friends, knew it, that was in love.
And during one break, I don't know.
They were putting their heads together and saying that one other girl, who was the most beautiful girl, a beautiful redhead girl, friend of mine, she met him the evening before.
And I was terribly upset because I knew that if Edith met him, I had no chance.
And so-- and then the bell rang and we went into the history lesson.
And we went.
And at the history lesson, the teacher-- I, I, I don't know.
I couldn't concentrate.
And I wrote down my sorrow on a piece of paper.
And, and the history teacher caught it.
And she read it out.
And she called me out.
And then she put something in, in our book.
And she sent me up to the headmistress.
And the headmistress called in my mother, said what a terrible person I am and what a terrible crime I committed.
I must say, later on, I became very friendly with this young man.
It wasn't worthwhile.
[LAUGHS] He was a very nice chap, but definitely not worthwhile being persecuted.
Because from that moment on by this history teacher, I was persecuted.
And, and every single time she came into the class, we had to get up and then sit down.
And the first thing, [NON-ENGLISH] get up, your lesson.
And I swotted the whole afternoon before history lesson.
And I couldn't get out a word.
I just went blank.
I didn't remember anything.
And she was the most anti-Semitic teacher altogether.
I mean, she was absol-- and she married, actually, the, the most-- the biggest anti-Semite in town, the leader of the-- the Arrow-- Arrow Cross.
In Hungary, Arrow Cross was what the swastika was in Germany.
Arrow Cross was in Hungary.
And he was the leader of the Arrow Cross band.
And she married him.
And she was an absolutely vicious woman.
And I was a nasty girl, because at the age of 15, I sent her a box, a dead rat in a box with a pink ribbon, and then another box and a red ribbon, and another box with a blue ribbon.
And I did it anonymously.
And in a way, I'm still ashamed of it, that I did it anonymously.
But I'm happy I did it.
So that's it.
But she was an absolutely-- and I, I, later on, I mean after the war, I checked up that this [INAUDIBLE] from-- this is French teacher.
I checked up, and she confirmed it that this woman was absolutely, viciously anti-Semitic.
Then our head teacher, our class head teacher, was anti-Semitic.
And the funny thing was that it turned out the she was actually a baptized Jewess.
And she was deported just the same.
But she was pretty anti-Semitic and pretty nasty as well.
And especially with me, because the other girls, they were Jewish.
I was-- I was an outsider.
My whole life, I was somehow an outsider.
I'm still an outsider in a way.
I, I was-- because I was always extremely outspoken.
And I always believed in the truth.
And you always say the truth.
And you shout it, not only say it.
And people didn't like it.
And you also shouted you are Jewish.
And in Hungary, you didn't talk about it.
I mean, if you were Jewish, you keep quiet about it.
It's not very nice to be Jewish.
I mean, one is Jewish.
And one keeps the festivals.
And one goes to synagogue.
But one doesn't speak about it.
But I did.
I didn't go to synagogue, but I did-- unless I had to-- but I did speak about my being Jewish.
And I stood up for it.
And anybody who attacked me, I, I really viciously attacked back because of that.
But and, and therefore, this teacher, of course, hated me because she hated my, my outspoken Jewishness.
I became, as I said, I was a Zionist.
At the age of 16, I was a Zionist leader.
And I was even sent to another town, which was called Sopron, which is also on the border of, of Hu-- of Austria, to open a new hall for the Zionists there, for the Zionist youth there.
And as I was standing there with a flag singing, the Hatik-- leading the singing of the Hatikvah, the police came in and took us all to the police headquarters and kept us for an hour.
I must say, we thought it was rather a joke.
But this was my first encounter with the police as a Jew, as a Zionist.
Yes, I suppose so.
I don't remember, really.
The only thing I remember that we were taken there.
And then I don't know why they let us go again.
The rest was-- well, as I say, at the age of 18 in 1942, I went up and did this course, this nursery school course.
When I finished, then the head of the nursery-- of the training college sent me out into various jobs, always about six weeks, two months.
And I spent the next year like that.
And she constantly changed my job.
I had lots of trouble with my father about it because he said, you are lazy.
You are not-- you don't-- you can't keep a job.
And it wasn't at all that.

Because eventually, I was then chosen in '44, January, I was chosen to become the assistant nursery school teacher at the training at the model nursery of the training college, of which I was incredibly proud.
And I was very, very pleased.
Unfortunately, not much came of it because this was January, 1944.
I also got ill then.
I had a funny illness that turned out I had a-- I had to have a big operation.
Anyway, I-- on the 19th of March 1944, the Germans marched in, and we were occupied.
Not-- I don't know whether it necessarily from the Austrians from what was going on.
The Hungarian were anti-Semitic.
They didn't need very much encouragement from the Germans.
They were anti-Semitic anyway, so that-- and they still are.
That was from '42 to '44.
I mean, it was only a one-year training course.
Yes.
In Budapest, I didn't feel any anti-Semitism at all, none whatsoever.
In my hometown, there always was anti-Semitism.
But I mean, my first memory of anti-Semitism was this when I was five.
That was really what, what triggered it off.
No, I knew.
We-- first of all, my father-- and he was also announced-- we had always a BBC World Service, which was absolutely forbidden.
But one of our maids did, did denounce him.
And my f-- yes, so that I knew what was going in the world.
And we still had letters.

Until '42, until Hungary declared the war to America and Russia and, and England, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that we had-- we had always-- I mean, we could correspond with my aunt who went first from Vienna to Brussels, and from Brussels to Marseille, and from Marseille to, to Mexico, and then to New York eventually.
And an uncle, the same, and my cousins-- they all went all over the world-- Australia, Philippines, Palestine.
They went all over the world.
Coming back still to the previous period, yes, to this period, I-- as a Zionist, I wanted to go to Palestine.
And I was with a group who, in 1942, went there when Hungary declared the war to Britain, to Great Britain.
We had our papers in for an affidavit at the British embassy.
And I had one paper missing.
And my group went.
And I couldn't go because I had one paper missing.
I don't know.
That I can't tell you because I don't remember.
I only know that I, I was very upset because I wanted to go with that group.
And the other thing was that I had one-- I tried later on going out illegally.
And I didn't manage.
I suppose it was-- well, after that, I still tried, from '42 onward.
I mean when, when, when I, I didn't manage to go legally, I tried illegally.
But I didn't manage.
Yet, I don't know.
I know I tried that.
And at the same time, I stopped.
Well, when I was in Budapest, I stopped going to the Zionists-- to Zionist because I just didn't have time.
And when I came home on holiday, I always took part still in the Zionist activities.
But I didn't take part in any Zionist activities from '42 to '44 because I just simply haven't had the time.
Yes, which was the 19th of March, 1944.
Well, by that time, I had this wonderful job.
It was very badly paid, but I was extremely proud to have got it.
And I lived-- at that point, I lived in a Jewish hostel, where, where we had-- which was an orthodox hostel, actually.
And I got in there because I became very friendly with two girls, two Czech girls, who it turned out after the 19th March that they were there illegally under false names.

And actually, in the hostel, practically all of the girls who were there were illegally-- were with illegal papers, with false papers, and came from Bratislava and from the various territories where people have been deported already.
Yes, they were Jewish, but they had false papers and lived under false names.
I became very friendly, and so I moved in with them.
And well, when the Germans came, then I was there in this.
And for the next four weeks, I spent practically in there.
Well, except the three days after the-- after the occupation, I was in hospital.
And I was operated with a perforated colon.
I was very, very ill.
And then four days afterwards-- this was in the Jewish hospital in Budapest.
And four days afterwards, the Germans threw out all the Jews from the hospital and took over the hospital, so that four days after this heavy operation, I was thrown out.
With the saying-- with the advice that I must keep a diet from then on my whole life.
I am afraid-- I mean, I'm afraid later on, I did keep a diet.
I didn't have anything to eat, so I kept a very good diet.
But I-- and, and I wasn't-- oh, yes.
I wasn't supposed to carry anything heavy, which I, unfortunately, I couldn't also keep to.
But I will talk about that consequently.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- my parents wanted me to come home after the-- when I came out from hospital.
And but we couldn't travel.
We had to have special permission.
And I had to wait.
I, I asked for permission.
I first had to make up my mind whether I really wanted to go home.
I had two offers of being hidden by, by non-Jews, of being hidden and also-- and being provided with false papers.
So I had a choice whether I go back or I was going to-- or I risk it, risk living with false papers and in hiding.
I decided to go back because at my last visit, I had a very big row with my father, and I wanted to make up with him.
I felt I wanted to make up with him.
So I went home.
And, and when I-- well, I had first asked for permission.
And the permission came on the 22nd of April 1944, which was a day before my 20th birthday.
And I phoned my father.
It-- that I-- it was a Saturday.
I phoned him that I'm coming home.
The permission was for me to travel on the 24th of April.
He, he was crying on the phone, really.
I mean, he used to be a very, very tough man who-- whom I could never kiss or never-- no emotions were shown.
And this time, he really cried.
And I said, well, I'm coming home.
And he said, well, we are going to celebrate your 20th birthday a day later.
And on the 24th, I went to-- I, I took the train.
And it was a very long journey.
And I was not permitted to sit next to anyone.
So I was only permitted to sit if there was an empty bench.
I was not permitted to speak to anyone or use the loo or use the restaurant car.
I had, beside my yellow star-- by the way, we had to wear yellow stars.
I mean, a few days after-- that was while I was in hospital, the yellow star came in.
So friends brought in yellow stars and sewed it on my clothing so that when I came out, I came out already with a yellow star.
And they, they came-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sorry, I lost my--
Yes, on the train.
And we had an air raid.
And we all had to get out because the train stopped.
We all had to get out and go onto the embankment and stay there.
And when we got back into the train, because of this air raid, the train came in five minutes after six o'clock in the evening.
After six in the evening, no Jews were supposed to be in the street.
That was one thing.
The second thing was no Jews were allowed to use a tram.
Now, with a tram, it would have taken me five to six minutes to get home.
We weren't very far.
But I had a heavy suitcase.
I had a heavy rucksack.
And I had my cello with me.
And with that, I could only move rather slowly.
So it took me about 20 minutes to get home.
And during that time, I was accosted.
I was called a dirty Jew.
I was spat at.
I was asked how dare I be in the street.
I had to show my permit, which was a very big yellow paper.
By the way, in the train, I also had a very big yellow, very broad yellow arm band on.
And this large paper, large yellow paper, which was my permit, I had to show to people.
Anybody could, could ask me and could stop me and ask for my permit.
So it was-- this was my homecoming.
It made it sure that I never was homesick again.
And I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and when I went-- got home, I found my mother, who was then 49 years old, and whom I saw three weeks earlier.
Not three weeks, but well I was-- no, now it was already seven weeks earlier-- I-- because I was at home.
That was my last time when I had this row with my father.
I had-- she-- she was 49.
And she looked still quite all right.
And when I came into the hou-- to the flat, I found a completely broken old woman there.
My mother was a completely confused, broken old woman.
All of the books were on the floor.
In my father's surgery, all the books, all the medical [INAUDIBLE], all the instrument, medical instruments, were all on the floor in, in a heap.
And my father wasn't there.
So when I asked what happened, the only thing I could get out of her was that they, they took him away after my phone conversation on the 22nd.
They came.
The Gestapo came with a Hungarian police and took him away.
And she hasn't heard since.
And she has no idea where he is and what has happened to him.
Now, that day, on the 22nd, they actually collected all the people who were pronounced socialists, or left-wing socialists.
So he was taken actually not as a Jew at that point, but as a socialist, as a political, definitely as a political prisoner.
And we had no more news of him.
This was the end.
We had not more news of him.
Yes, I couldn't.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Then, I-- well, as I say, my mo-- my mother was, she just couldn't make any decisions.
She couldn't.
She was completely confused.
And so I, I got-- somehow I sorted things out.
A few days later, we had to had in our jewels.
And we had to hand in our money-- our bank, savings bank books.
Well, bicycles went first.
I think so.
And then-- And all that, well, these were orders.
And you couldn't do anything against it.
We-- then on the 7th of May-- so this was 24th.
I came back on the 24th of April.
The 7th of May, we had to move in to the ghetto, which were two streets hermetically sealed off with barbed wire.
And we were, in a way, quite lucky because we were two and we were moved in with a family of four.
So we're only six in the room.
There was only one bed, but we slept on the floor.
Well, that is a different story.
Yes, fine.
Because I must then tell about what happened to the Hungarian Jews, Jewish men, which, which is a very important and interesting point.
It was my mother.
And we were in the ghetto.
We were women, children, and men over 50.
Now I, perhaps I come to that point about my brother.
Oh, I see.
